edition: Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute
description: Modern legal terms from Cornell Law's Wex Legal Dictionary — focused on digital rights, government accountability, and civil rights

terms.definition: The federal statute, 5 U.S.C. § 552, requiring federal agencies to make records available to any person upon request, subject to nine specified exemptions including national security, deliberative process, and personal privacy.

Federal law establishing procedures for physical and electronic surveillance of foreign powers and their agents within the United States; creates the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court (FISC) for approval of surveillance orders.

Constitutional protection against unreasonable searches and seizures; requires probable cause supported by oath for issuance of warrants. Extended to digital data in Carpenter v. United States and Riley v. California.

Technology that uses optical character recognition to capture images of license plates and automatically compare them to databases; generates records of vehicle location at specific times, raising Fourth Amendment concerns when used by government.

Physiological or behavioral characteristics used for automated identification including fingerprints, facial geometry, iris patterns, voiceprints, and gait; subject to specialized state privacy laws (BIPA) and Fourth Amendment protections when collected by government.

A recording device worn by law enforcement officers to document their interactions with the public; policies governing activation, retention, and public access vary by jurisdiction and raise transparency and civil rights issues.

A device that mimics a cell tower to force nearby mobile phones to transmit identifying information; also called Stingray or IMSI catcher. Warrant requirement confirmed in several federal circuits; raises Fourth Amendment concerns.

The government seizure of property alleged to be connected to criminal activity, without a criminal conviction; proceeds typically flow to the seizing agency creating revenue incentives. Subject to Eighth Amendment excessive fines analysis.

Rights guaranteed to individuals by the Constitution and federal statutes, including equal protection, due process, freedom from unreasonable searches, and protections against discrimination; enforceable against state actors under 42 U.S.C. § 1983.

Acting under the authority of state or local government; a § 1983 plaintiff must show the defendant acted under color of state law. Includes action taken under authority of ordinance, regulation, custom, or usage of a state.

A court-approved settlement agreement resolving claims of civil rights violations or regulatory non-compliance; typically includes injunctive relief, monitoring requirements, and reporting obligations. Enforceable as a court order.
terms.citation: United States v. City of Ferguson, E.D. Mo. 2016

terms.definition: A civil claim under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 for deprivation of constitutional rights by state actors; requires showing (1) deprivation of a right secured by the Constitution, (2) by a person acting under color of state law.

Organizational rules governing how long electronic data, records, and communications must be preserved; government agencies are subject to statutory retention schedules; failure to follow policies may constitute spoliation.

Information from which all direct and indirect personal identifiers have been removed such that re-identification risk is low; de-identification does not eliminate all privacy concerns, particularly for location data and biometrics.

Electronic data that may be used in legal proceedings, including emails, text messages, metadata, GPS records, server logs, and surveillance footage; requires proper chain of custody, authentication, and compliance with search warrant requirements.

A facially neutral policy or practice that has a disproportionate adverse effect on a protected class; actionable under Title VII, the Fair Housing Act, and Title VI without proof of discriminatory intent.
terms.citation: 42 U.S.C. § 2000e; Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U.S. 424

terms.definition: A directive issued by the President or a state governor with the force of law; must be grounded in statutory or constitutional authority; subject to judicial review. Federal executive orders are published in the Federal Register.

Biometric software analyzing facial geometry to identify or verify individuals from images or video; used by law enforcement and government agencies; accuracy disparities across demographic groups raise equal protection concerns.

The doctrine that federal law supersedes conflicting state law pursuant to the Supremacy Clause; may be express, implied by field occupation, or implied by conflict. Federal spending conditions may also preempt inconsistent state practices.

Constitutional protection of freedom of speech, press, religion, assembly, and petition; applies to government censorship and compelled speech. Content-based restrictions are subject to strict scrutiny; content-neutral restrictions to intermediate scrutiny.

A warrant ordering a technology company to identify all devices located within a defined geographic area during a specified time period; raises Fourth Amendment third-party doctrine and particularity concerns.

The obligation of public officials and agencies to answer for their exercise of public power; enforced through audits, oversight, public records laws, and judicial review; foundational to democratic governance.

A legal instrument reflecting a relationship between a federal or state award agency and a recipient, in which the principal purpose is transfer of money or property to carry out a public purpose; subject to conditions attached by the grantor.

A requirement attached to receipt of federal or state funds; conditions must be unambiguous, directly related to the federal interest, and not constitute coercion; compliance is subject to audit and may result in disallowance of costs.
terms.citation: South Dakota v. Dole, 483 U.S. 203; 2 C.F.R. Part 200

terms.definition: An examination of financial statements or compliance with laws and regulations by a qualified auditor who is independent of the audited entity; required for federal grant recipients under the Single Audit Act.

The First Amendment protection for journalists' confidential sources and work product; recognized in varying degrees by federal and state shield laws; limits government subpoenas seeking journalist's sources.

Courts require clear congressional authorization for agency actions of vast economic or political significance; applied in West Virginia v. EPA (2022) to limit EPA's Clean Power Plan; increasingly invoked against expansive agency surveillance and data programs.

Municipal liability under § 1983 for constitutional violations caused by an official policy, widespread custom, or deliberate indifference to training needs; requires that the municipality's policy or custom be the moving force behind the violation.
terms.citation: Monell v. Department of Social Services, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

terms.definition: A warrant authorizing law enforcement to enter a premises without prior announcement of their presence; disfavored due to safety risks and Fourth Amendment concerns; subject to constitutional reasonableness requirements.

The constitutional prohibition on Congress delegating its legislative power to administrative agencies without an intelligible principle to guide the delegation; the Court has signaled renewed interest in limiting agency authority under this doctrine.
terms.citation: A.L.A. Schechter Poultry Corp. v. United States, 295 U.S. 495 (1935)

terms.definition: State statutes requiring governmental bodies to conduct their deliberations and decisions in public; prohibiting secret meetings except for specified executive session purposes; violations may render decisions voidable.
terms.citation: Government in the Sunshine Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552b

terms.definition: State public records statutes analogous to federal FOIA requiring government agencies to make records available for public inspection and copying; vary in scope, exemptions, and enforcement mechanisms.
terms.citation: 5 U.S.C. § 552; Cal. Gov. Code § 7920 et seq.

terms.definition: A course of governmental conduct constituting a systemic violation of civil rights; actionable by the Department of Justice under 42 U.S.C. § 14141 to require reform of police and corrections agencies.

A device or process that records or decodes dialing, routing, addressing, or signaling information transmitted by an instrument or facility; requires court order under 18 U.S.C. § 3121; subject to Third-Party Doctrine analysis.

The application of statistical and data analysis tools to forecast criminal activity and direct law enforcement resources; subject to civil rights scrutiny for disparate impact, transparency requirements, and accuracy standards.
terms.citation: ACLU v. Clearview AI; Santa Cruz Ordinance 2020

terms.definition: The requirement that government means be proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued; applied in Fourth Amendment reasonableness analysis, civil rights remedies, and administrative penalties; excessive measures are subject to constitutional challenge.

A judicially created doctrine protecting government officials from civil liability for constitutional violations unless the right was clearly established at the time of the conduct; shielding officials from suits where prior cases did not put the constitutional question beyond debate.
terms.citation: Harlow v. Fitzgerald, 457 U.S. 800 (1982); Pearson v. Callahan, 555 U.S. 223

terms.definition: A schedule specifying how long types of government records must be preserved before they may be destroyed; required by the Federal Records Act for federal agencies; forms the backbone of government records management and audit trail preservation.

The public's right to access information held by government; encompasses FOIA rights, state open records rights, and constitutional protections for access to judicial proceedings; foundational to governmental accountability.
terms.citation: 5 U.S.C. § 552; Nixon v. Warner Communications, 435 U.S. 589

terms.definition: 42 U.S.C. § 1983, the Reconstruction-era civil rights statute providing a civil remedy against any person who, acting under color of state law, deprives another of rights secured by the Constitution or federal law.

Federal statute requiring non-federal entities that expend $750,000 or more in federal awards in a fiscal year to obtain an annual single audit covering financial statements and compliance with federal program requirements.

Acquisition of goods or services from a single vendor without competitive solicitation; justified by unique capabilities, proprietary rights, urgency, or other circumstances; requires documented justification and approval authority.

Article I, Section 8, Clause 1 authority allowing Congress to spend for the general welfare with conditions; conditions must be unambiguous, related to the federal interest, and not coercive; source of federal grant programs and accompanying compliance requirements.
terms.citation: U.S. Const. art. I, § 8, cl. 1; South Dakota v. Dole, 483 U.S. 203

terms.definition: A brand name for a cell-site simulator used by law enforcement to identify mobile devices in a geographic area; requires warrant in most federal circuits after Carpenter v. United States; subject to non-disclosure agreements limiting transparency.

Electronic means of monitoring, tracking, recording, or analyzing individuals' activities, communications, or locations; subject to Fourth Amendment warrant requirements when used by government; increasingly subject to local oversight ordinances.

The Fourth Amendment principle that information voluntarily shared with third parties is not protected; significantly curtailed by Carpenter v. United States for comprehensive cell-site location information.
terms.citation: Smith v. Maryland, 442 U.S. 735 (1979); Carpenter v. United States, 585 U.S. 296

terms.definition: Section of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibiting discrimination on the basis of race, color, and national origin in programs and activities receiving federal financial assistance; compliance is a condition of all federal grants and subject to audit.

A public disclosure by a government agency or technology company of information about data requests, surveillance orders, access demands, or other activities affecting privacy; promotes accountability for surveillance programs.

An agency's proactive release of records or information without a formal FOIA request; encouraged under FOIA and open government policies; reduces information asymmetry between government and the public.

Legal protection for individuals who report government misconduct, fraud, waste, or abuse; federal protections include the Whistleblower Protection Act, False Claims Act, and Inspector General Act; state equivalents vary in scope.

The rule from Carpenter v. United States (2018) requiring a warrant for government access to historical cell-site location information spanning seven or more days; marks a significant limitation on the third-party doctrine for comprehensive digital surveillance.

Federal statute imposing civil liability on those who submit false claims to the government; provides for qui tam actions by private whistleblowers (relators) who may recover a portion of any recovery; primary anti-fraud enforcement tool for government contracts and grants.

A provision allowing a private person (relator) to file suit on behalf of the government to recover damages for fraudulent claims against the government; the relator receives a portion of any recovery as an incentive to expose fraud.

The constitutional guarantee, found in the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments, that the government may not deprive any person of life, liberty, or property without fair procedures (procedural due process) and without sufficient justification under substantive law (substantive due process). The applicable procedures are determined by balancing private interest, risk of erroneous deprivation, and government interest.
terms.citation: U.S. Const. amend. V, XIV; Mathews v. Eldridge, 424 U.S. 319 (1976)
terms.relevance_to_audit: Core constitutional constraint on government action; any audit finding involving denial of benefits, license revocation, or punitive action against an individual or entity without notice and hearing implicates due process.
